was a private, [Christian](/wiki/Christian_college "Christian college"), [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts "Liberal arts") college that was located in [Cape May](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). It was involved in a landmark case requiring religious schools to acquire a state license to grant academic degrees.

Supreme Court of New Jersey (1982). ["New Jersey Board of Higher Education v. Shelton College"](http://law.justia.com/cases/new-jersey/supreme-court/1982/90-n-j-470-0.html). Retrieved 24 April 2017.

See Russell Kirk, "Shelton College and State Licensing of Religious Schools: An Educator's View of the Interface Between the Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses," *Law & Contemporary Problems*, 44:2 (Spring 1981), 169-184 [Kirk's article is excellent in what it asserts, but some historical details are in need of correction]. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Private religious college in New Jersey

Shelton College

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Former name | National Bible Institute |
| Active | 1907 (1907)–1991 (1991) |
| [Principal](/wiki/Principal_(academia) "Principal (academia)") | [J. Oliver Buswell](/wiki/J._Oliver_Buswell "J. Oliver Buswell") |
| Location | [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") , United States |

**Shelton College** was a private, [Christian](/wiki/Christian_college "Christian college"), [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts "Liberal arts") college that was located in [Cape May](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). It was involved in a landmark case requiring religious schools to acquire a state license to grant academic degrees.

The college motto was *"Training Christian Warriors."*[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shelton_College&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Shelton College was founded by Don Odell Shelton in 1907 as the National Bible Institute of New York City, and it was incorporated in 1908.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Harden-3) The Union Missionary Training Institute of Brooklyn, founded by Lucy D. Osborn in 1885, merged with the National Bible Institute in 1916.[[3]](#cite_note-Harden-3) From 1925 to 1952 the National Bible Institute's headquarters were located at 340 West 55th Street in New York City and was known as the [National Bible Institute School and Dormitory](/wiki/National_Bible_Institute_School_and_Dormitory_(New_York_City) "National Bible Institute School and Dormitory (New York City)").

[Carl McIntire](/wiki/Carl_McIntire "Carl McIntire") was instrumental in the leadership of the college from the early 1940s until it closed in 1991.

The National Bible Institute was renamed as Shelton College in 1950.[[3]](#cite_note-Harden-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Testimony-4) The college moved to a campus in [Ringwood, New Jersey](/wiki/Ringwood,_New_Jersey "Ringwood, New Jersey"), in 1953,[[4]](#cite_note-Testimony-4) then to [Cape May, New Jersey](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey") in 1963.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1971 the college moved to [Cape Canaveral](/wiki/Cape_Canaveral "Cape Canaveral"), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), and then back to Cape May in 1979.

In September 1973, McIntire became Chancellor.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In *[New Jersey Board of Higher Education v. Shelton College](/wiki/New_Jersey_Board_of_Higher_Education_v._Shelton_College "New Jersey Board of Higher Education v. Shelton College")*, the [Supreme Court of New Jersey](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_Jersey "Supreme Court of New Jersey") forbade Shelton from granting degrees without a state license.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The school became a certificate granting institution until it closed in 1992.

In 2014, the roof collapsed and the building was demolished.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Academic programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shelton_College&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Academic programs")]

* Bachelor of Arts (BA)
* Bachelor of Sacred Theology (STB)
* Bachelor of Theology (BTh)
* Bachelor of Divinity (BD)
* Bachelor of Religious Education (BRE)
* Master of Religious Education (MRE)

## Notable alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shelton_College&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable alumni")]

* [Edwin M. Yamauchi](/wiki/Edwin_M._Yamauchi "Edwin M. Yamauchi"), historian
* [Spiros Zodhiates](/wiki/Spiros_Zodhiates "Spiros Zodhiates"), author and Biblical scholar
* [Ronald E. Manahan](/wiki/Ronald_E._Manahan "Ronald E. Manahan"), president of Grace College and Theological Seminary
* [Timothy Tow](/wiki/Timothy_Tow "Timothy Tow"), principal of [Far Eastern Bible College](/wiki/Far_Eastern_Bible_College "Far Eastern Bible College")

## Notable faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shelton_College&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notable faculty")]

* [Jack Murray](/wiki/John_W._Murray "John W. Murray"), President
* [Arthur E. Steele](/wiki/Arthur_E._Steele "Arthur E. Steele"), President
* [Francis Nigel Lee](/wiki/Francis_Nigel_Lee "Francis Nigel Lee"), Theology
* [Virginia Ramey Mollenkott](/wiki/Virginia_Ramey_Mollenkott "Virginia Ramey Mollenkott"), English
* [Gary G Cohen](/wiki/Gary_G._Cohen "Gary G. Cohen"), Physics

## Publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shelton_College&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Publications")]

Shelton College publishes a theological journal, *The Bible Today*.

## Leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shelton_College&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Leadership")]

| President |
| --- |
| * Don Odel Shelton (1907 - 29 Jan 1941) * [J. Oliver Buswell](/wiki/J._Oliver_Buswell "J. Oliver Buswell") (15 May 1941 - 1955) * [John W. Murray](/wiki/John_W._Murray "John W. Murray") (1955 - 1960) * [Clyde J. Kennedy](/wiki/Clyde_J._Kennedy "Clyde J. Kennedy") (1960 - 1962) * [Arthur E. Steele](/wiki/Arthur_E._Steele "Arthur E. Steele") (1962 - 1965) * [Carl McIntire](/wiki/Carl_McIntire "Carl McIntire") (1965 - 1972) * Hyland W. Shepherd (1972 - 1977) * Irwin W. Steele (Acting) (1977 - 1978) * Glenn Rogers (1978 - 1982) * Dennis J. M. Brown (1982 - ) |
